seo.title: Repo Monkey | GitHub-native userscript control for Chrome
seo.description: Repo Monkey turns GitHub repositories into a modern workflow for syncing, editing, and shipping browser userscripts.

hero.eyebrow: MV3 extension for deliberate script shipping
hero.title: Turn browser scripts into a disciplined release system.
hero.lead: Repo Monkey connects your userscripts to GitHub so every change can be synced, reviewed, edited, and shipped from a cleaner control room inside Chrome.

primaryPanel.label: Repository cockpit
primaryPanel.title: A versioned workspace for scripts that used to live as loose snippets.
primaryPanel.body: Keep the habits you already trust: branches, history, structured editing, and a single source of truth for automation code.

points.one: Bind a GitHub repo and load scripts from `/output/` or the repo root.
points.two: Review local changes before they become the live browser version.
points.three: Sync on demand or let the extension keep your script catalog up to date.

secondaryPanel.label: Runtime bridge
secondaryPanel.title: A calmer control room inside Chrome.
secondaryPanel.body: Enable scripts, inspect their state, and keep deployment friction low without giving up Git-backed discipline.

features.title: Built for people who want scripts to behave like real project assets.
features.lead: The site is opinionated because the product is opinionated: version control, better visibility, and fewer copy-paste rituals.

repo.title: Repository-first
repo.body: Bind one GitHub repository and let Repo Monkey treat your scripts as versioned assets instead of scattered browser-side state.

sync.title: Structured sync
sync.body: Bring remote scripts into the extension, compare local changes, and keep a stable source of truth for what should run.

edit.title: Focused editing
edit.body: Work in a purpose-built extension interface that highlights script state, local modifications, and sync conflicts clearly.

ship.title: Safer release path
ship.body: Use branches, diffs, and commit-ready thinking to move from experiment to live script with less risk.

workflow.title: A four-step loop for syncing and shipping userscripts.
workflow.lead: The extension keeps the workflow simple while preserving the discipline of a code repository.

connect.title: Bind your repository
connect.body: Add a GitHub personal access token and point the extension at `owner/repo`, HTTPS, or SSH format.

sync.title: Sync the catalog
sync.body: Pull scripts from `/output/` when present, otherwise from the repository root, and keep the list ready in Chrome.

edit.title: Edit with visibility
edit.body: Inspect script status, update code locally, and keep modified or conflicted states visible before shipping.

release.title: Run and release
release.body: Enable the scripts you need, confirm browser permissions, and promote changes with more confidence.

scenarios.title: Designed for power users, maintainers, and teams that automate in the browser.
scenarios.lead: Repo Monkey works best when userscripts matter enough to deserve traceability, reviewability, and a repeatable release flow.

clean.label: The UI stays focused on script operations.

solo.title: Solo builders
solo.body: Keep personal automation organized instead of losing track of one-off scripts spread across files and browser storage.

team.title: Shared script libraries
team.body: Store scripts in a repository that teammates can inspect, review, and evolve without opaque handoffs.

ops.title: Operational tooling
ops.body: Use userscripts for dashboards, internal tools, and repetitive workflows while keeping changes easier to reason about.

faq.title: What the product does and how to get it running.
faq.lead: A few details matter when you wire browser automation to a repository-backed workflow.

what.question: What exactly is Repo Monkey?
what.answer: It is a Chrome MV3 extension that manages userscripts through a GitHub repository instead of treating them as isolated browser snippets.

where.question: Where should my scripts live?
where.answer: The extension looks in `/output/` first. If that directory does not exist, it falls back to loading `.js` files from the repository root.

permission.question: What browser setup is required?
permission.answer: You still need to enable the Chrome "Allow User Scripts" permission so scripts synced from your repository can execute.

who.question: Who is this best for?
who.answer: People who already think in repositories, diffs, and release hygiene, and want the same discipline for browser-side automation.

cta.title: Build a cleaner userscript pipeline on top of GitHub.
cta.body: If your scripts deserve version history and a sharper release loop, Repo Monkey gives the browser a control room that feels closer to real software delivery.

footer.copy: Repo Monkey for disciplined browser automation.
